content: Let $P, Q, R$ and $S$ be the attributes of a relation in a relational schema. Let $X \rightarrow Y$ indicate functional dependency in the context of a relational database, where $X, Y \subseteq\{P, Q, R, S\}$ Which of the following options is/are always true?
explanation:
Let $P, Q, R, S$ be attributes of a relation.

Check each statement one by one using functional dependency rules.

$\{P Q \rightarrow R, P \rightarrow R\} \Rightarrow Q \rightarrow R$ : False

This is not always true because knowing $Q$ alone does not give $R$.

From the given dependencies, we can find the closure of $Q$.

                                                               $$ Q^{+}=\{Q\} $$

Since $R$ is not in $Q^{+}$, we cannot say $Q \rightarrow R$.

$\{P Q \rightarrow R\} \Rightarrow\{P \rightarrow R$ or $Q \rightarrow R\}$ : False

This is also not always true.

If $P Q \rightarrow R$, it means both $P$ and $Q$ together determine $R$.

It does not mean that $P$ alone determines $R$, or $Q$ alone determines $R$.

For example, the closure of $P$ is only:

                                                               $$ P^{+}=\{P\} $$

So $P \rightarrow R$ is not implied. Similarly, $Q \rightarrow R$ is also not implied.

$\{P \rightarrow R, Q \rightarrow S\} \Rightarrow\{P Q \rightarrow R S\}:$ True

This is true.

From $P \rightarrow R$, if we know $P$, we get $R$.

From $Q \rightarrow S$, if we know $Q$, we get $S$.

So if we know both $P$ and $Q$, then we can get both $R$ and $S$.

Hence, $P Q \rightarrow R S$.

                                                               $$ [P Q]^{+}=P Q R S $$

$P \rightarrow R \Rightarrow P Q \rightarrow R$ : True

This is true by augmentation.

If $P$ determines $R$, then adding $Q$ with $P$ on the left side still keeps the dependency true.

So $P Q \rightarrow R$.

                                                               $$ [P Q]^{+}=P Q R $$

Therefore, the always true statements are the third and fourth options.

content: In the context of relational database normalization, which of the following statements is/ are true?

options.content: It is always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving 3NF decomposition of a relation
It is always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving 1NF decomposition of a relation
It is not always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving BCNF decomposition of a relation
It is not always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving 2NF decomposition of a relation

explanation:
Option A

"It is always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving 3NF decomposition of a relation"

This is true.

By the standard 3NF decomposition algorithm, for any relation schema, we can always get a lossless and dependency-preserving decomposition into 3NF.

So, Option A is true.

Option B

"It is always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving 1NF decomposition of a relation"

This is true.

1NF is the most basic normal form, requiring only atomic values. Any relation can be represented in 1NF, and since we can even keep the relation as it is, dependency preservation is not a problem.

So, Option B is true.

Option C

"It is not always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving BCNF decomposition of a relation"

This is true.

A BCNF decomposition can always be obtained, but it may not preserve all functional dependencies. Thus, dependency preservation is not always possible in BCNF.

So, Option C is true.

Option D

"It is not always possible to obtain a dependency-preserving 2NF decomposition of a relation"

This is false.

Since 3NF decomposition is always possible with dependency preservation, and every 3NF relation is also in 2NF, a dependency-preserving 2NF decomposition is certainly possible.

So, Option D is false.

Final Answer

The correct statements are:

$$ \boxed{A,\ B,\ C} $$

content: Which of the following statements about a relation $R$ in first normal form (1NF) is/are TRUE?

options.content: $R$ cannot have more than one candidate key

explanation:
To determine which statements about a relation $R$ in first normal form (1NF) are true, let's first understand what 1NF entails. A relation is in 1NF if it adheres to the following rules:

  • All columns must contain atomic (indivisible) values.
  • Each column must contain values of the same type.
  • Each column must contain a unique name.
  • The order in which data is stored does not matter.

With this understanding, let's analyze each option:

Option A: $R$ can have a multi-attribute key

True. A multi-attribute key, also known as a composite key, is allowed in 1NF. A composite key is simply a key that consists of more than one attribute, and there is no restriction in 1NF against having such keys.

Option B: $R$ cannot have a foreign key

False. The concept of 1NF does not exclude the use of foreign keys. Foreign keys are related to establishing relationships between tables, but 1NF only concerns the atomicity of the values in each column.

Option C: $R$ cannot have a composite attribute

True. A composite attribute consists of multiple sub-parts and is not atomic. Since 1NF requires that each attribute contain atomic values, composite attributes are not allowed.

Option D: $R$ cannot have more than one candidate key

False. There is no restriction in 1NF on the number of candidate keys. A relation in 1NF can have multiple candidate keys.

Therefore, the correct statements about a relation $R$ in first normal form (1NF) are:

Option A: $R$ can have a multi-attribute key

Option C: $R$ cannot have a composite attribute

content: In a relational data model, which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: A relation with only two attributes is always in BCNF.
If all attributes of a relation are prime attributes, then the relation is in BCNF.
Every relation has at least one non-prime attribute.

explanation:
Relation with 2 attributes always in BCNF

R(AB) {A $$\to$$ B} $$\Rightarrow$$ BCNF

{B $$\to$$ A} $$\Rightarrow$$ BCNF

{A $$\to$$ B, B $$\to$$ A} $$\Rightarrow$$ BCNF

{No non-trivial FD's} $$\Rightarrow$$ BCNF

$$\bullet$$ If all prime attributes than relation always in 3NF but may not BCNF.

$$\bullet$$ Not mandatory of atleast one non-prime attribute in RDBMS table.

$$\bullet$$ Not every relation can decompose into BCNF with dependency preserving.

content:
Consider a relation R(A, B, C, D, E) with the following three functional dependencies.

AB $$\to$$ C ; BC $$\to$$ D ; C $$\to$$ E;

The number of superkeys in the relation R is _________.
explanation:
AB+ = ABCDE

$$ \therefore $$ AB : Candidate key

Non prime attribute = C, D, E

$$ \therefore $$ Number of superkeys = 23 = 8. Those are AB, ABC, ABD, ABE, ABCD, ABCE, ABDE, ABCDE. In super key candidate key should always present.

content:
Consider the following statements S1 and S2 about the relational data model:

S1: A relation scheme can have at most one foreign key.

S2: A foreign key in a relation scheme R cannot be used to refer to tuples of R.

Which one of the following choices is correct?

options.content: S1 is true and S2 is false.
Both S1 and S2 are true.
Both S1 and S2 are false.
S1 is false and S2 is true.

explanation:
To determine which statements are correct, let's analyze each one individually in the context of the relational data model.

Statement S1:

A relation scheme can have at most one foreign key.

Analysis:

Definition of Foreign Key:

A foreign key is a set of one or more attributes in a relation (table) that refers to the primary key or a candidate key in another relation (or possibly the same relation).

The purpose of a foreign key is to enforce referential integrity between data in two relations.

Possibility of Multiple Foreign Keys:

A relation can have multiple foreign keys, each referencing a primary key in the same or different relations.

Example:

Employee table:

Attributes: EmployeeID (PK), Name, DepartmentID (FK to Department), ManagerID (FK to Employee).

Here, the Employee table has two foreign keys:

DepartmentID references the Department table.

ManagerID references the Employee table itself (self-referencing foreign key).

Conclusion:

A relation scheme can have multiple foreign keys.

Statement S1 is false.

Statement S2:

A foreign key in a relation scheme $ R $ cannot be used to refer to tuples of $ R $.

Analysis:

Self-Referencing Foreign Keys:

A foreign key can reference the primary key of the same relation. This is known as a self-referencing or recursive foreign key.

Use Cases:

Hierarchical Data:

Representing organizational structures, such as an employee-manager relationship.

Bill of Materials:

Items that are composed of other items in a nested fashion.

Example:

Employee table:

Attributes: EmployeeID (PK), Name, ManagerID (FK to EmployeeID).

ManagerID is a foreign key that references EmployeeID in the same table, establishing a relationship where an employee can be the manager of other employees.

Conclusion:

A foreign key can refer to tuples within the same relation.

Statement S2 is false.

Final Answer:

Statement S1 is false.

Statement S2 is false.

Answer: Option C

Both S1 and S2 are false.

content:
A database of research articles in a journal uses the following schema. 
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE, TITLE, YEAR, PRICE)
The primary key is (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE) and the following functional dependencies exist in the schema. 
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE) → TITLE
(VOLUME, NUMBER) → YEAR
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE) → PRICE
The database is redesigned to use the following schemas. 
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE, TITLE, PRICE)
(VOLUME, NUMBER, YEAR)
Which is the weakest normal form that the new database satisfies, but the old one does not?

Consider the relation $$X\left( {P,Q,R,S,T,U} \right)$$ with the following set of functional dependencies
$$\eqalign{ & F = \left\{ \, \right. \cr & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left\{ {P,R} \right\} \to \left\{ {S,T} \right\}, \cr & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left\{ {P,S,U} \right\} \to \left\{ {Q,R} \right\} \cr & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left. \, \right\} \cr} $$
Which of the following is the trivial functional dependency in $${F^ + },$$ where $${F^ + }$$ is closure of $$f$$ ?

Consider the relation schema $$R = \left( {E,\,F,\,G,\,H,\,I,\,J,\,K,L,\,M,\,N} \right)$$ and the set of functional dependencies $$\left\{ {\left\{ {E,F} \right\} \to \left\{ G \right\},\left\{ F \right\}} \right.$$
$$ \to \left\{ {I,J} \right\},\left\{ {E,H} \right\} \to \left\{ {K,L} \right\},\left\{ K \right\}$$
$$ \to \left\{ M \right\},\left\{ L \right\}$$
$$ \to \left. {\left\{ N \right\}} \right\}$$ on $$R.$$ What is the key for $$R?$$
A prime attribute of a relation scheme $$R$$ is an attribute that appears

options.content: Only in the primary key of $$R.$$

A table has fields, $$F1, F2, F3, F4, F5,$$ with the following functional dependencies:
$$F1 \to F3.\,F2 \to F4.\,\,\,\left( {F1\,.\,F2} \right) \to F5$$ in terms of Normalization, this table is in
Which one of the following statements about normal forms is FALSE?

options.content: Lossless, dependency $$-$$ preserving decomposition into $$3NF$$ is always possible
Lossless, dependency - preserving decomposition into $$BCNF$$ is always possible
Any relation with two attributes is in $$BCNF$$

content: Relation $$R$$ with an associated set of functional dependencies, $$F,$$ is decomposed into $$BCNF.$$ The redundancy (arising out of functional dependencies) in the resulting set of relations is

content: Consider a schema $$R(A,B,C,D)$$ and functional dependencies $$A \to B\,\,$$ and $$C \to D\,\,$$. Then the decomposition of $$R$$ into $${R_1}\left( {AB} \right)$$ and $${R_2}\left( {CD} \right)$$ is

options.content: Lossless join but not dependency preserving
Dependency preserving but not lossless join
Not dependency preserving and not lossless join

content: Let $$R=(A,B,C,D,E,F)$$ be a relation scheme with the following dependencies: $$C \to F,\,E \to A,\,EC \to D,\,A \to B.$$ Which of the following is a key for $$R?$$
Which normal form is considered adequate for normal relational database design?
State True or False with reason. There is always a decomposition into Boyce-codd normal form $$(BCNF)$$  that is lossless and dependency preserving.

In the context of schema normalization in relational DBMS, consider a set $F$ of functional dependencies. The set of all functional dependencies implied by $F$ is called the closure of $F$. To compute the closure of $F$, Armstrong's Axioms can be applied. Consider $X, Y$, and $Z$ as sets of attributes over a relational schema. The three rules of Armstrong's Axioms are described as follows.

Reflexivity: If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$

Augmentation: If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $X Z \rightarrow Y Z$ for any $Z$

Transitivity: If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$

The additional rule of Union is defined as follows.

Union: If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Y Z$

It can be proved that the additional rule of Union is also implied by the three rules of Armstrong's Axioms. Listed below are four combinations of these three rules. Which one of these combinations is both necessary and sufficient for the proof?
explanation:
Reflexivity, if $Y \subseteq X$ then $X \rightarrow Y$

Augmentation, if $X \rightarrow Y$ then $X Z \rightarrow Y Z$

Transitivity, if $X \rightarrow Y, Y \rightarrow Z$ then $X \rightarrow Z$

The additional union rule

If $X \rightarrow Y, X \rightarrow Z$ then $X \rightarrow Y Z$

To derive union rule required Augmentation or transitivity.

If $X \rightarrow Y$ then $X \rightarrow X Y$ : Augmentation

If $X \rightarrow Z$ then $X Y \rightarrow Y Z:$ Augmentation

If $X \rightarrow X Y, X Y \rightarrow Y Z$ then $X \rightarrow Y Z:$ Transitivity.

content: Consider a relational database schema with a relation $R(A, B, C, D)$. If $\{A, B\}$ and $\{A, C\}$ are the only two candidate keys of the relation $R$, then the number of superkeys of relation $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ . (answer in integer)
explanation:
$\{\underline{A B}, \underline{A C}\}$ candidate keys.

Number of super keys.

$$ \Rightarrow \quad 2^2+2^2-2=6 $$

6 superkeys

content:
Consider the following relational schema along with all the functional dependencies that hold on them.

$$\begin{aligned} & R 1(A, B, C, D, E):\{D \rightarrow E, E A \rightarrow B, E B \rightarrow C\} \\ & R 2(A, B, C, D):\{A \rightarrow D, A \rightarrow B, C \rightarrow A\} \end{aligned}$$

Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE?
explanation:
$R_1(A B C D E)$ $R_2(A B C D)$
$\{D \rightarrow E, E A \rightarrow B, E B \rightarrow C\}$ $\{A \rightarrow D, A \rightarrow B, C \rightarrow A\}$
$(D A)^{+}=\{D E A B C\}$ $C^{+}=C A B D$
$$\underline {AD} $$ : Candidate key $$\underline {C} $$ : Candidate key
$R_1$ in 1 NF but not in 2NF $R_2$ in 2 NF but not in 3 NF

content:
Consider a relational schema team(name, city, owner), with functional dependencies \{name $\rightarrow$ city, name $\rightarrow$ owner}.

The relation team is decomposed into two relations, $t 1$ (name, city) and $t 2$ (name, owner). Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE?

options.content: The relation team is NOT in BCNF.
The relations $t 1$ and $t 2$ are in BCNF.
The decomposition constitutes a lossless join.
The relation team is NOT in 3NF.

explanation:
In the relational schema team(name, city, owner), we have the functional dependencies:

name → city

name → owner

The candidate key for this relation is name, since it determines all other attributes (city and owner). Therefore, the relation team is in BCNF because every determinant is a candidate key.

The relation team is decomposed into two new relations:

T1(name, city)

T2(name, owner)

Both T1 and T2 are in BCNF because each has a single functional dependency where the determinant is a candidate key (name → city in T1 and name → owner in T2).

The decomposition of the team into T1 and T2 is lossless join, which means that when T1 and T2 are joined back together using a natural join on the name attribute, we can recreate the original team relation without any loss of information.

Additionally, the decomposition is dependency preserving, meaning that all functional dependencies are preserved in the decomposed relations.

Overall, the original relation team is in BCNF, and both decomposed relations (T1 and T2) are also in BCNF. The decomposition is both lossless and dependency preserving.

content:
A functional dependency $F: X \to Y$ is termed as a useful functional dependency if and only if it satisfies all the following three conditions:

  • $X$ is not the empty set.
  • $Y$ is not the empty set.
  • Intersection of $X$ and $Y$ is the empty set.

For a relation $R$ with 4 attributes, the total number of possible useful functional dependencies is _________
explanation:
To determine the total number of possible useful functional dependencies for a relation $R$ with 4 attributes, we need to carefully analyze the conditions specified for a functional dependency $F: X \to Y$ to be considered useful.

Let's denote the 4 attributes by $A, B, C, D$.

A useful functional dependency $F: X \to Y$ must satisfy the following conditions:

  • $X$ is not the empty set.
  • $Y$ is not the empty set.
  • The intersection of $X$ and $Y$ is the empty set (i.e., $X \cap Y = \emptyset$).

Given that the relation $R$ has 4 attributes, there are a total of $2^4 - 1 = 15$ possible non-empty subsets for $X$. Similarly, there are $2^4 - 1 = 15$ possible non-empty subsets for $Y$. However, we must ensure that $X \cap Y = \emptyset$.

The total number of useful functional dependencies can be determined by counting all valid combinations of $X$ and $Y$ such that $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint subsets of $\{A, B, C, D\}$.

Let's approach this step by step:

  1. We need to choose $X$ such that it's a non-empty subset of $\{A, B, C, D\}$. Each non-empty subset can be chosen in $2^4 - 1 = 15$ ways.
  2. For a given $X$, $Y$ must be a non-empty subset of the remaining attributes $\{A, B, C, D\} \setminus X$. The number of attributes left after choosing $X$ is $4 - |X|$.
  3. The number of non-empty subsets of the remaining attributes is $2^{4-|X|} - 1$.
  4. We need to sum these counts over all possible sizes of $X$.

Summarizing, we compute the total number of useful functional dependencies:

$$ \sum_{k=1}^{4} \binom{4}{k} \cdot (2^{4-k} - 1) $$

Where $\binom{4}{k}$ represents the number of ways to choose $X$ with $k$ attributes.

Calculating each term:

  • For $k=1$: $\binom{4}{1} \cdot (2^{4-1} - 1) = 4 \cdot (8 - 1) = 4 \cdot 7 = 28$
  • For $k=2$: $\binom{4}{2} \cdot (2^{4-2} - 1) = 6 \cdot (4 - 1) = 6 \cdot 3 = 18$
  • For $k=3$: $\binom{4}{3} \cdot (2^{4-3} - 1) = 4 \cdot (2 - 1) = 4 \cdot 1 = 4$
  • For $k=4$: $\binom{4}{4} \cdot (2^{4-4} - 1) = 1 \cdot (1 - 1) = 1 \cdot 0 = 0$

Adding all these, the total number of useful functional dependencies is:

$$ 28 + 18 + 4 + 0 = 50 $$

Thus, the total number of possible useful functional dependencies is 50.

content: The symbol → indicates functional dependency in the context of a relational database. Which of the following options is/are TRUE?

options.content: ((X, Y) → Z and W → Y) implies (X, W) → Z
(X → Y and Y → Z) implies X → Z

explanation:
Option A:

$$(X, Y) \rightarrow (Z, W)$$ implies $$(X \rightarrow (Z, W)).$$

This is not always true. Knowing that the pair (X, Y) determines (Z, W) does not necessarily mean that X alone determines (Z, W).

Option B:

$$(X, Y) \rightarrow (Z, W)$$ implies $$(X, Y) \rightarrow Z.$$

This is true. If the pair (X, Y) determines (Z, W), it certainly determines Z individually as Z is part of (Z, W).

Option C:

$$((X, Y) \rightarrow Z \text{ and } W \rightarrow Y)$$ implies $$((X, W) \rightarrow Z).$$

This is true due to the principle of transitivity in functional dependencies.

Option D:

$$(X \rightarrow Y \text{ and } Y \rightarrow Z)$$ implies $$X \rightarrow Z.$$

This is true by the transitive property of functional dependencies.

Thus, the options that are TRUE are:

Option B, Option C, and Option D.

content:
Suppose the following functional dependencies hold on a relation U with attributes P, Q, R, S, and T :

P → QR

RS → T

Which of the following functional dependencies can be inferred from the above functional dependencies?
explanation:
Given Functional Dependencies :

$ P \rightarrow Q R $

This means $ P $ functionally determines both $ Q $ and $ R $.

$ R S \rightarrow T $

This means the combination of $ R $ and $ S $ functionally determines $ T $.

Option A : $ P \rightarrow R $

Analysis :

From the given dependency $ P \rightarrow Q R $, we can decompose it into :

$ P \rightarrow Q $

$ P \rightarrow R $

Therefore, $ P \rightarrow R $ can be directly inferred.

Conclusion : Option A is correct.

Option B : $ P S \rightarrow T $

Analysis :

We need to check if $ P S \rightarrow T $ can be inferred using the given dependencies.

From $ P \rightarrow R $ :

Since $ P \rightarrow R $ and $ P S $ includes $ P $, we can augment $ P \rightarrow R $ with $ S $ :

$ P S \rightarrow R S $

From $ R S \rightarrow T $ :

We already have $ R S \rightarrow T $.

Transitivity :

Using transitivity on the above results :

If $ P S \rightarrow R S $ and $ R S \rightarrow T $, then $ P S \rightarrow T $.

Conclusion : Option B is correct.

Option C : $ R \rightarrow T $

Analysis :

We need to determine if $ R \rightarrow T $ can be inferred from the given dependencies.

Given : $ R S \rightarrow T $

To infer $ R \rightarrow T $, we would need either :

$ S \rightarrow T $, or

$ R \rightarrow S $

But we have no such dependencies.

No inference rule allows us to deduce $ R \rightarrow T $ from $ R S \rightarrow T $ alone.

Conclusion: Option C cannot be inferred and is incorrect.

Option D : $ P S \rightarrow Q $

Analysis :

From $ P \rightarrow Q $ :

Since $ P \rightarrow Q $ and $ P S $ includes $ P $, we can augment $ P \rightarrow Q $ with $ S $ :

$ P S \rightarrow Q $

Conclusion: Option D is correct.

Final Answer :

Options A, B, and D can be inferred from the given functional dependencies.

Option C cannot be inferred.

Therefore, the functional dependencies that can be inferred are :

Option A : $ P \rightarrow R $

Option B : $ P S \rightarrow T $

Option D : $ P S \rightarrow Q $

Answer: Options A, B, and D can be inferred from the given functional dependencies.

content:
Consider the relation R(P, Q, S, T, X, Y, Z, W) with the following functional dependencies.

PQ → X; P → YX; Q → Y; Y → ZW

Consider the decomposition of the relation R into the constituent relations according to the following two decomposition schemes.

D1 : R = [(P, Q, S, T); (P, T, X); (Q, Y); (Y, Z, W)]

D2 : R = [(P, Q, S); (T, X); (Q, Y); (Y, Z, W)]

Which one of the following options is correct?

options.content: D1 is a lossy decomposition, but D2 is a lossless decomposition.
Both D<sub>1</sub> and D<sub>2</sub> are lossless decompositions.
Both D<sub>1</sub> and D<sub>2</sub> are lossy decompositions.
D<sub>1</sub> is a lossless decomposition, but D<sub>2</sub> is a lossy decomposition.

explanation:
To determine whether the decompositions $ D_1 $ and $ D_2 $ are lossless or lossy, we'll analyze each decomposition using the concept of lossless join decomposition and the given functional dependencies (FDs).

Given Relation and Functional Dependencies:

Relation $ R $: $ (P, Q, S, T, X, Y, Z, W) $

Functional Dependencies:

$ PQ \rightarrow X $

$ P \rightarrow YX $

$ Q \rightarrow Y $

$ Y \rightarrow ZW $

Decomposition $ D_1 $:

$ R = \{ R_1(P, Q, S, T); \ R_2(P, T, X); \ R_3(Q, Y); \ R_4(Y, Z, W) \} $

Checking for Lossless Join:

Step 1: Identify the common attributes between the relations.

Between $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $: Common attributes are $ \{ P, T \} $.

Step 2: Check if the common attributes form a superkey in one of the relations.

Compute $ (P, T)^+ $:

Start with $ \{ P, T \} $.

From $ P \rightarrow YX $, add $ Y, X $.

From $ Y \rightarrow ZW $, add $ Z, W $.

Closure: $ (P, T)^+ = \{ P, T, Y, X, Z, W \} $.

In Relation $ R_2(P, T, X) $:

Attributes are $ \{ P, T, X \} $.

Since $ (P, T)^+ $ includes all attributes of $ R_2 $, $ \{ P, T \} $ is a superkey for $ R_2 $.

Conclusion for $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $: Since the common attributes $ \{ P, T \} $ form a superkey for $ R_2 $, the join between $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $ is lossless.

Similarly, for other pairs:

    Between $ R_1 $ and $ R_3 $: Common attribute is $ Q $.

    $ Q \rightarrow Y $, but $ (Q)^+ = \{ Q, Y \} $ does not cover $ R_1 $, but $ Q $ is a superkey for $ R_3 $.

    Conclusion: The join between $ R_1 $ and $ R_3 $ is lossless.

      Between $ R_3 $ and $ R_4 $: Common attribute is $ Y $.

      $ Y \rightarrow ZW $, so $ (Y)^+ = \{ Y, Z, W \} $.

      $ Y $ is a superkey for $ R_4 $.

      Conclusion: The join between $ R_3 $ and $ R_4 $ is lossless.

      Final Conclusion for $ D_1 $:

      All joins where relations share common attributes are lossless.

      **Therefore, $ D_1 $ is a *lossless* decomposition.**

      Decomposition $ D_2 $:

      $ R = \{ R_1(P, Q, S); \ R_2(T, X); \ R_3(Q, Y); \ R_4(Y, Z, W) \} $

      Checking for Lossless Join:

      Between $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $:

      Common attributes: None ($ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $ are disjoint).

      Conclusion: Cannot have a lossless join between $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $.

      Between $ R_2 $ and other relations:

      $ R_2(T, X) $ does not share common attributes with any other relation except possibly through dependencies.

      Problem Identified:

      No common attributes between $ R_2 $ and other relations to facilitate a lossless join.

      Functional dependencies involving $ T $ and $ X $:

      No FDs are given that involve $ T $ alone determining $ X $ or vice versa.

      Thus, $ R_2 $ lacks a superkey, and the decomposition does not preserve dependencies or guarantee a lossless join.

      Final Conclusion for $ D_2 $:

      **$ D_2 $ is a *lossy* decomposition** because there is no way to guarantee that the original relation can be reconstructed without loss of information.

      Answer:

      Option D is correct:

      $ D_1 $ is a lossless decomposition, but $ D_2 $ is a lossy decomposition.

      Answer: Option D

content: Consider a relational table R that is in 3NF, but not in BCNF. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: A cell in R holds a set instead of an atomic value.
R has a nontrivial functional dependency X $$ \to $$ A, where X is not a superkey and A is a non-prime attribute and X is not a proper subset of any key
R has a nontrivial functional dependency X $$ \to $$ A, where X is not a superkey and A is a non-prime attribute and X is a proper subset of some key.
R has a nontrivial functional dependency X $$ \to $$ A, where X is not a superkey and A is a prime attribute.

explanation:
To determine which statement is TRUE, let's analyze each option in the context of a relational table $ R $ that is in Third Normal Form (3NF) but not in Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF).

Understanding the Difference Between 3NF and BCNF:

3NF Definition:

A relation $ R $ is in 3NF if, for every nontrivial functional dependency $ X \to A $:

(a) $ X $ is a superkey, or

(b) $ A $ is a prime attribute (an attribute that is part of some candidate key).

BCNF Definition:

A relation $ R $ is in BCNF if, for every nontrivial functional dependency $ X \to A $:

$ X $ is a superkey.

Key Point: The main difference is that 3NF allows certain functional dependencies where $ X $ is not a superkey, provided $ A $ is a prime attribute. BCNF is stricter and does not allow any such exceptions.

Analyzing the Options:

Option A:

Statement: A cell in $ R $ holds a set instead of an atomic value.

Analysis:

First Normal Form (1NF): Requires that all attributes contain only atomic (indivisible) values.

Since $ R $ is in 3NF, it must also be in 1NF.

Conclusion: False. $ R $ cannot have non-atomic values.

Option B:

Statement: $ R $ has a nontrivial functional dependency $ X \to A $, where $ X $ is not a superkey and $ A $ is a non-prime attribute, and $ X $ is not a proper subset of any key.

Analysis:

In 3NF, if $ X $ is not a superkey, then $ A $ must be a prime attribute.

This option states that $ A $ is a non-prime attribute, which would violate 3NF.

Conclusion: False. Such a dependency would prevent $ R $ from being in 3NF.

Option C:

Statement: $ R $ has a nontrivial functional dependency $ X \to A $, where $ X $ is not a superkey and $ A $ is a non-prime attribute, and $ X $ is a proper subset of some key.

Analysis:

Similar to Option B, $ A $ is a non-prime attribute.

In 3NF, this is not allowed when $ X $ is not a superkey.

The fact that $ X $ is a subset of a key does not change this violation.

Conclusion: False. Such a dependency would violate 3NF.

Option D:

Statement: $ R $ has a nontrivial functional dependency $ X \to A $, where $ X $ is not a superkey and $ A $ is a prime attribute.

Analysis:

This situation is exactly what differentiates 3NF from BCNF.

In 3NF, such a dependency is allowed.

In BCNF, it is not allowed because $ X $ must be a superkey.

Therefore, $ R $ is in 3NF but not in BCNF.

Conclusion: True.

Final Answer:

Option D accurately describes a scenario where $ R $ is in 3NF but not in BCNF.

Answer: Option D

R has a nontrivial functional dependency X → A, where X is not a superkey and A is a prime attribute.

content:
Let the set of functional dependencies

F = {QR → S, R → P, S → Q}

hold on a relation schema X = (PQRS). X is not in BCNF. Suppose X is decomposed into two schemas Y and Z, where Y = (PR) and Z = (QRS).

Consider the two statements given below.

I. Both Y and Z are in BCNF
II. Decomposition of X into Y and Z is dependency preserving and lossless

Which of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation:
To determine which statements are correct, let's analyze each statement step by step.

Given:

Relation Schema: $ X = \{P, Q, R, S\} $

Functional Dependencies (F):

$ QR \to S $

$ R \to P $

$ S \to Q $

Decomposition:

$ Y = \{P, R\} $

$ Z = \{Q, R, S\} $

Statement I: Both $ Y $ and $ Z $ are in BCNF.

Checking BCNF for $ Y $:

Find Candidate Keys for $ Y $:

Attributes: $ P, R $

Relevant FD: $ R \to P $

Closure of $ R $:

$ R^+ = \{ R, P \} $ (since $ R \to P $)

Therefore, $ R $ is a candidate key for $ Y $.

Check Functional Dependencies:

FD: $ R \to P $

Left side $ R $ is a candidate key.

No violation of BCNF.

Conclusion for $ Y $:

$ Y $ is in BCNF.

Checking BCNF for $ Z $:

Find Candidate Keys for $ Z $:

Attributes: $ Q, R, S $

Relevant FDs:

$ QR \to S $

$ S \to Q $

Find Closures:

$ QR^+ $:

$ QR \to S $, so $ QR^+ = \{ Q, R, S \} $

$ QR $ is a candidate key.

$ RS^+ $:

$ S \to Q $, so $ RS^+ = \{ R, S, Q \} $

$ RS $ is a candidate key.

Check Functional Dependencies:

FD: $ QR \to S $

Left side $ QR $ is a candidate key.

No violation of BCNF.

FD: $ S \to Q $

Left side $ S $ is not a candidate key.

Violates BCNF.

Conclusion for $ Z $:

$ Z $ is not in BCNF.

Conclusion for Statement I:

Statement I is FALSE because $ Z $ is not in BCNF.

Statement II: Decomposition of $ X $ into $ Y $ and $ Z $ is dependency preserving and lossless.

Checking for Lossless Join:

Common Attribute: $ R $ (present in both $ Y $ and $ Z $)

Condition for Lossless Join:

The common attribute must be a superkey in at least one of the relations.

In $ Y $:

$ R $ is a candidate key.

Condition satisfied.

Conclusion:

Decomposition is lossless.

Checking for Dependency Preservation:

Functional Dependencies in Decomposed Schemas:

In $ Y $:

$ R \to P $

In $ Z $:

$ QR \to S $

$ S \to Q $

All original FDs are preserved in the decomposed schemas.

Conclusion:

Decomposition is dependency preserving.

Conclusion for Statement II:

Statement II is TRUE.

Final Answer:

Statement I: FALSE

Statement II: TRUE

Answer: Option C

II only

Consider an Entity-Relationship (ER) model in which entity sets E1 and E2 are connected by an m : n relationship R12. E1 and E3 are connected by a 1 : n (1 on the side of E1 and n on the side of E3) relationship R13.

E1 has two single-valued attributes a11 and a12 of which a11 is the key attribute. E2 has two single-valued attributes a21 and a22 of which a21 is the key attribute. E3 has two single-valued attributes a31 and a32 of which a31 is the key attribute. The relationships do not have any attributes.

If a relational model is derived from the above ER model, then the minimum number of relations that would be generated if all the relations are in 3NF is _______.

Let $$R\left( {A,B,C,D} \right)$$ be a relational schema with the following functional dependencies:
$$A \to B,\,\,B \to C,\,\,C \to D$$ and $$D \to B.$$
The decomposition of $$R$$ into $$(A,B), (B,C)$$ and $$(B,D)$$

options.content: gives a lossless join, and is dependency preserving
gives a lossless join, but is not dependency preserving.
does not give a lossless join, but is dependency preserving
does not give a lossless join and is not dependency preserving

content: Which one of the following statements if FALSE?

options.content: Any relation with two attributes is in $$BCNF.$$
A relation in which every key has only one attribute is in $$2NF$$.
A prime attribute can be transitively dependent on a key in a $$3$$ $$N$$F relation.
A prime attribute can be transitively dependent on a key in a $$BCNF$$ relation.

content:
Consider the relation enrolled (student, course) in which (student, course ) is the primary key, and the relation Paid (student, amount) where student is the primary key. Assume no null values and no foreign keys or integrity constraints. Assume that amounts $$6000, 7000, 8000,9000$$ and $$10000$$ were each paid by $$20$$% of the students. Consider these query plans (Plan $$1$$ on left, Plan $$2$$ on right) to ''List all courses taken by students who have paid more than $$x''$$.

A disk seek takes $$4ms$$, disk data transfer bandwidth is $$300MB/s$$ and checking a tuple to see if amount is greater than $$x$$ takes $$10\mu s.$$ Which of the following statements is correct?

options.content: Plan $$1$$ and Plan $$2$$ will not output identical row sets for all databases
A course may be listed more than once in the output of plan $$1$$ for some databases
For $$x=5000,$$ Plan $$1$$ executes faster than plan $$2$$ for all databases
For $$x=9000,$$ Plan $$1$$ executes slower than plan $$2$$ for all databases

(a) Insert into $$R$$ (b) Insert into $$S$$
(c) Delete from $$R$$ (d) Delete from $$S$

Which of the following statements is true about the referential integrity constraint above?

All of (a), (b), (c) and (d) can cause its violation
